zones.name: Src
insights: 12 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
The provider-interface + provider-registry pattern establishes a solid open/closed extension point — new vendors can be added without modifying core client logic.
Token usage tracking is correctly co-located with the provider layer rather than in utilities, keeping billing concerns close to the transport boundary.
The bidirectional import with src-2 (3 imports src→src-2) is worth watching — ideally this foundational layer should have zero upward dependencies into the utility layer.
The 3 imports from this zone into src-2 (utilities) creates a mild coupling inversion — the foundation layer should not depend on the utility layer above it.
Cohesion of 0.6 is acceptable for a multi-provider abstraction; the provider registry and session files bind otherwise loosely related provider implementations together.

The 3 outgoing imports into llm-cli-utilities mean the foundational provider layer has a runtime dependency on the utility layer above it — any change to the utility API can break the provider core without a test catching it, since the test zone covers core but not utilities.

The 88 incoming calls from the test zone combined with 0 outgoing test calls to utilities creates a protection asymmetry: the core is well-tested but the utilities it depends on are not, so the 3 upward imports are doubly risky.

llm-provider-core imports 3 symbols from llm-cli-utilities while llm-provider-tests provides no coverage for those utility symbols — the 3 upward dependency edges are untested by the existing test suite.

The 4 outgoing external calls (to node built-ins or npm packages) combined with 88 incoming from tests suggests the core is correctly thin on external deps — the 3 utility imports stand out as the only inward-leaning coupling.

The 3 upward imports into llm-cli-utilities almost certainly originate from create-client.ts or a provider constructor needing runtime config (project dirs, auth tokens) — the dependency is driven by initialization concerns leaking into the transport layer rather than being injected at construction time.

The 12-entry-point surface amplifies the impact of the bidirectional coupling: every upward import from utilities has 12 possible re-export paths it could travel through, making it harder to trace which public symbols carry the layering violation.

12 exported entry points on a foundation layer that is imported by 23 edges from utilities and 25 from tests creates an oversized public contract — changes to any of the 12 exports can ripple into both consumer zones simultaneously. Reduce to 1–2 entry points aligned with the src/public.ts convention used across the monorepo.

create-client.ts and the provider constructors importing runtime config or auth from llm-cli-utilities indicates initialization concern leakage — providers should receive config values via constructor injection rather than pulling them from a higher-level utility layer, eliminating the 3 reverse imports structurally.

codex-cli-provider.ts encodes a vendor name in a file that sits alongside transport-named peers (api-provider, cli-provider) — the naming convention mixes vendor identity with transport strategy at the file level, making it unclear whether future vendors should be named by vendor (openai-cli-provider) or by transport (http-provider).

llm-types.ts is generically named in a zone scoped to provider concerns — if this file contains package-wide shared types (token structs, config shapes) rather than provider-only types, its placement in provider-core is the structural root cause of the 3 reverse imports: core holds types that utilities need, so utilities import core to get them rather than importing a neutral types zone.

provider-session.ts implies stateful session management at the transport layer, but auth credentials live in auth.ts in llm-cli-utilities with no visible contract between the two — session construction likely requires auth context, making the session↔auth coupling implicit and untraceable across zone boundaries.

Rename codex-cli-provider.ts to follow the transport-first naming pattern used by api-provider and cli-provider — e.g. codex-provider.ts or openai-cli-provider.ts depending on whether the file abstraction is vendor or transport. Mixing both in the filename breaks the zone's naming convention and will compound as more vendors are added.

Audit llm-types.ts to determine whether it holds provider-scoped or package-wide types. If it contains types shared with llm-cli-utilities (project dir shapes, token structs, provider config interfaces), move those types to a new zero-dependency types module — this is likely the direct cause of the 3 reverse imports and fixing the placement fixes the cycle structurally.

The 12 entry-point warning (src/0) is a heuristic threshold crossing but has no corroborating LLM finding. With a public.ts convention confirmed in global finding 2, the wide surface may be intentional delegation. Do not treat this as critical without additional evidence.

The 12 entry points from automated heuristic analysis (src/0) and the 'single public entry point' claim from LLM pass 1 (global/2) are in direct tension. Both cannot be simultaneously true at the intra-package zone level — either the src zone has one file imported from outside (supporting global/2) or twelve (supporting src/0). This discrepancy has not been reconciled in any subsequent pass.

Findings src/0 (12 entry points, automated heuristic) and global/2 (single public entry point pattern, LLM pass 1) contradict each other. The automated heuristic counts files in the src zone that have incoming import edges from other zones; if 12 such files exist, the src/public.ts single-entry-point convention is not being honored at the zone boundary level. Audit which of the 12 src zone files are imported directly by src-2 zone files and determine whether those imports should be consolidated to route through a single gateway or public.ts re-export.

Code inspection confirms api-provider.ts, cli-provider.ts, auth.ts, create-client.ts, and llm-client.ts all import from types.ts, llm-types.ts, provider-interface.ts, and config.ts. If those type/config files are classified into the src-2 zone by Louvain clustering, these are the edges producing the bidirectional coupling count. The imports themselves are architecturally correct (services depending on types), so the coupling metric reflects zone misclassification, not a logic error.

Audit the 12 files in the src zone that have incoming import edges from src-2. Cross-reference each against src/public.ts exports to determine whether the import is correctly surfaced through the package entrypoint or bypasses it. Priority files to check: types.ts, llm-types.ts, provider-interface.ts, and config.ts — all confirmed as direct imports from api-provider.ts, cli-provider.ts, auth.ts, and create-client.ts. If these type/config files are the source of all 12 entry points, the finding reflects zone boundary placement rather than an API surface violation.

src/0 (12 entry points, call-graph-derived) and global/2 (single entry-point pattern, LLM-inferred) describe different scopes: global/2 observes the public.ts convention's intent at the package boundary; src/0 counts intra-package cross-zone import edges, which are expected within a single package. Both can be simultaneously correct — the contradiction is terminological. global/2 should not be relied upon to disprove src/0.

Before treating the 12 entry-point count as a public API violation, confirm whether the edges are intra-package (src-2 files importing from src files within the same package) or cross-package (external consumers bypassing public.ts). Intra-package cross-zone imports are architectural zone concerns, not public API violations, and do not require remediation via public.ts consolidation. Only cross-package direct imports need to be routed through public.ts.

The single-entry-point surface (config.ts) combined with 19 files means the utilities zone has a wide internal surface but a narrow public contract — callers cannot easily reach internal helpers directly.

The 269 internal calls with only 4 incoming calls from outside the zone means the utility layer is almost entirely self-serving — the single public entry point (config.ts) hides a densely tangled internal graph that would resist decomposition if the zone ever needs to be split.

Auth, config, project-dirs, and exec are four distinct concerns currently merged into one zone; each has a different change driver (security policy, user preferences, filesystem layout, subprocess behavior) — co-locating them creates a zone where any concern's change can accidentally break the others.

auth.ts handles credential management but lives in an undifferentiated utilities zone alongside exec, output, and json helpers — there is no isolation boundary preventing other utilities from importing auth internals, making credential-handling code invisible in security audits of the zone.

The zone contains 19 files spanning at least four distinct concerns (auth, config, CLI presentation, filesystem/exec) with 269 internal calls indicating deep internal entanglement. A zone this wide with this many internal edges will resist future splitting — the four concern groups should be separated before entanglement grows further.

help-format.ts is a pure UI presentation utility co-located with credential management (auth.ts) — these have orthogonal change drivers (visual formatting vs security policy) and orthogonal audit scopes, yet they share a zone with no sub-boundary separating them.

The zone name 'llm-cli-utilities' uses the llm- prefix, but all 19 files inside it (auth, config, exec, output, json, project-dirs) drop this prefix entirely — there is a systematic naming discontinuity between zone identity and file identity that would make a new contributor unsure which zone a new file belongs to.

Internal call-graph cohesion of 1.0 in a 19-file zone spanning 4 distinct concerns indicates false cohesion — the files are entangled by mutual imports rather than unified by a single concept. High call-graph cohesion in a wide zone is a warning sign of accidental coupling, not intentional design.

Separate help-format.ts and output.ts (presentation concerns) from auth.ts and exec.ts (security and subprocess concerns) with at minimum a directory-level boundary within the zone. Mixing UI formatting and credential handling in an undifferentiated file set means a security audit of auth.ts must also consider whether output.ts inadvertently logs credentials.

Establish a file-naming prefix or directory grouping convention that maps visibly to the zone name — e.g. group files under src/config/, src/auth/, src/cli/ subdirectories. The current flat structure with a zone name that does not appear in any filename makes zone membership invisible to contributors and tooling.

The combination of 42% file concentration, generic name, and being the destination of 23 upward import crossings makes this zone the highest-priority candidate for renaming and internal boundary documentation — not splitting, since global finding 3 confirms four zones is appropriate granularity.

Manually assign a domain-specific name to this zone (e.g. 'llm-cli-utilities') in the sourcevision configuration. The automated enrichment failure combined with the zone's size means every consumer reading the architecture map gets no signal about what this zone does. A name is zero-cost and eliminates the ambiguity that compounds the architectural risks in global findings 8 and 11.

Zone name ambiguity and enrichment failure are independently confirmed by two separate mechanisms (size heuristic and LLM domain-label attempt), making the naming gap more certain than either signal alone. A manual name paired with a short zone README is the minimum viable fix — it costs nothing and eliminates the ambiguity that compounds every downstream risk in this zone.

With 13+ test files covering distinct CLI commands, confirm each file maps 1:1 to a CLI command module to maintain navigability as the command surface grows.
Absence of any entry points is correct for a test-only zone; ensure the vitest/jest config explicitly targets this directory so tests are never accidentally excluded from CI runs.
Cohesion 1.0 and coupling 0 confirm the e2e suite is architecturally isolated — an excellent baseline that should be preserved as new commands are added.

The breadth of CLI commands covered (adapter, analyze, import, prune, quiet, recommend, smart-add, sync plus 5 more) indicates strong end-to-end coverage; cross-reference with the CLI command list to identify any untested commands.
This zone is the only one with perfect cohesion in the entire codebase — a sign that the e2e layer has well-maintained boundaries compared to production zones.

The cli-e2e-tests zone (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0) exercises CLI commands that internally invoke notion-integration and integration-schemas logic, yet the e2e zone has zero direct coupling to either integration zone — this confirms the integration layer is correctly hidden behind CLI command abstractions, a clean layering win

cli-e2e-tests achieves coupling 0 despite covering adapter-related CLI commands (cli-adapter.test.ts, cli-sync.test.ts) — the integration layer is fully encapsulated behind CLI boundaries, which is the correct abstraction and should be preserved as the integration surface grows

cli-e2e-tests achieves coupling 0 by testing integration behavior exclusively through CLI stdout/exit-code assertions — this constrains test depth to observable CLI behavior only, meaning integration regressions that produce correct output but corrupt internal state (e.g. malformed prd.json after a sync) are structurally undetectable by this suite

cli-recommend.test.ts is the e2e test for the highest-risk command in the codebase (recommend.ts is the only command that bridges the analysis and persistence layers directly), yet the e2e suite can only assert on CLI stdout/exit-code — it cannot verify that acknowledged-findings.json was correctly written or that the PRD was not corrupted after a recommendation run. The most structurally complex command has the weakest test observability of any command in the suite.

Add a post-command file-state assertion to cli-recommend.test.ts: after running the recommend command, read acknowledged-findings.json and assert on its structure. This is the minimum additional coverage needed to catch persistence regressions in the highest-risk command without adding a full integration test layer.

zones.name: Integration Schemas
zones.description: Defines and exports the Zod/type schemas that describe the data shapes exchanged with external integrations (Jira, Notion) at the store boundary.
insights: High coupling (0.69) — 3 imports target "remote-integration"
Grouping Jira and Notion schemas under a shared index.ts barrel is a clean pattern — changes to either integration's schema have a single, obvious location.

Cohesion of 0.31 is below ideal; the Jira and Notion schemas are peers with no internal cross-dependency, so the community algorithm grouped them purely by shared consumers rather than internal coupling — this is acceptable for a schema barrel but worth noting.
Ensure the index.ts re-exports are kept minimal and explicit; schema barrels that grow unchecked tend to expose internal draft types and create hidden coupling for consumers.
Coupling 0.69 is high for a schema-only zone — schemas are being imported by many upstream modules, which is expected but means any schema shape change will have broad blast radius.

Cohesion 0.31 reflects two largely independent schema files grouped by external consumers rather than internal structure; this is architecturally acceptable for a schema barrel but indicates the zone boundary is consumer-driven, not domain-driven.

notion.ts lives here while the adapter and mapping logic live in the notion-integration zone — consider collocating or at minimum documenting that these two zones together form the full Notion integration layer.

integration-schemas is a pure-data zone (no logic, only shape declarations) while notion-integration is a behavioral zone (adapter + mapping logic) — this data/behavior split across zones is architecturally asymmetric and means the schema barrel is tightly coupled to the behavioral zone's consumers without being co-located with the code that interprets those schemas

integration-schemas (pure schema types) is consumed by remote-integration (3 imports) but has no import link to notion-integration despite both serving the same external service — schema and adapter are decoupled from each other while both couple to separate internal zones, a leaky abstraction at the service boundary

The index.ts barrel in integration-schemas re-exports both Jira and Notion schemas, but the Notion adapter lives in a separate zone with no import from this barrel — the barrel does not serve its natural consumer, undermining its purpose as a unified schema surface

integration-schemas does not appear as a source in cross-zone import traffic despite index.ts existing specifically to be consumed — this absence suggests the barrel's consumers are within the same algorithmic zone or consume it through file-level imports that bypass zone boundaries, making the barrel's actual downstream reach unverifiable from zone-level data alone

The index.ts barrel re-exports both jira.ts and notion.ts schemas but notion-integration (the natural consumer of notion.ts) has no import link to this barrel — the barrel's primary consumer for Notion schema types is some zone other than the adapter that interprets those schemas, inverting the expected schema→adapter data-flow

jira.ts has no counterpart behavioral zone — unlike Notion, which has notion-integration to house adapter and mapping logic, Jira has schema types only with no adapter zone anywhere in the codebase. If Jira adapter code is added, it will have no natural home and will likely accrete into whichever existing zone is most convenient at the time, repeating the fragmentation pattern of the Notion integration.

Pre-emptively create a jira-integration zone directory (src/store/jira/) before any Jira adapter code is written. Establishing the zone boundary before the code exists prevents the organic fragmentation that produced the notion-integration vs integration-schemas split. This is a process recommendation, not a code change.

schema-validation is the only zone that contains tests from three different abstraction levels (unit, integration, e2e) alongside a production file. The other production zones (file-analysis-pipeline, zone-detection-engine) contain only same-level (unit) tests. This implicit convention — production zones may co-locate unit tests but not integration or e2e tests — is followed everywhere except schema-validation, indicating the violation is accidental rather than intentional and should be codified as an explicit rule.

The zone name 'schema-validation' accurately describes the production file (validate.ts) but is simultaneously an accurate description of what the co-located tests do. This naming coincidence means the zone name provides no signal about whether a given file is production or test code, further obscuring the boundary.

schema-validation is the only zone with both high coupling (0.57) AND low cohesion (0.43) AND mixed-abstraction-level test membership simultaneously. Prioritize decomposing this zone above the others.

Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm these fixtures are fully isolated from production code, which is exactly the right design — they should never import from the sourcevision source tree.

The fixture projects cover two important archetypes (a file-router Remix app and a plain TypeScript project); consider adding a third fixture representing a monorepo or a project with circular imports to stress-test edge cases in the zone-detection and import analyzers.
Fixture files should be treated as immutable test data — avoid modifying them during test runs and ensure they are excluded from the production build output.
Cohesion of 1.0 and coupling of 0.0 represent the ideal profile for a test-data zone; this isolation should be enforced by lint rules preventing any production file from importing fixture paths.

The Remix fixture includes nested route files (_auth.login.tsx, users.$id.tsx) which exercise route-detection.ts; verify the fixture remains up to date with Remix v2 file-system routing conventions to keep e2e tests meaningful.

Having only two fixture projects limits coverage of edge cases such as barrel-heavy codebases or projects with no imports; expanding the fixture library would strengthen confidence in Louvain zone detection accuracy.

No cross-zone import path reaches these fixtures from any production zone, which is correct, but the inverse — whether any production code path conditionally references fixture paths via string literals or require() — should be verified to confirm runtime isolation matches import-graph isolation.
This zone is the only zone in the package with both perfect cohesion and zero coupling — it serves as the cleanliness baseline against which other zones' test discipline can be measured.

Import-graph isolation is confirmed, but runtime isolation (no production code referencing fixture paths via dynamic strings) has not been verified — a lint rule banning fixture path strings in src/ would close this gap.

test-fixture-projects (cohesion: 1.0, coupling: 0.0) is the only zone whose health is guaranteed to remain stable regardless of refactoring elsewhere — its zero coupling means no external change can affect its metrics. The fixture zone's perfect isolation should be used as the measurable baseline in CI: if any future analysis run shows coupling > 0 for this zone, it is an unambiguous signal that a production file has gained a runtime dependency on fixture paths — a critical boundary violation detectable before any test failure occurs.

The CLI command + core next-task logic are cohesive; the apparent low cohesion is an artifact of test files having many external imports. Production-level call graph shows cohesion 0.95, coupling 0.05. No refactoring would reduce coupling without splitting tests from source.

Structurally sound — the zone boundary correctly groups the feature's implementation and tests.

After absorbing recommend.ts and sync.ts commands, the zone has a clear purpose: all user-facing write operations.
